# AI Description

# Technical Description of Figure A-4.2.2.3.-A

## 1. Diagram Type
This is a technical detail drawing consisting of two views: a section view and a plan view, illustrating the geometric determination of primary and secondary sidelighted areas for daylighting calculations in accordance with NECB requirements.

## 2. Building Components and Systems Shown
- Vertical glazing (windows) in exterior walls
- Primary sidelighted floor areas
- Secondary sidelighted floor areas
- Vertical obstructions (1.5 m or higher)
- Opaque breaks between windows
- Head height (HH) measurement reference points

## 3. Visible Labels, Measurements, and Annotations

### Section View (Top):
- "top of glazing" - indicates the upper edge of the window
- "head height (HH)" - vertical dimension from floor to top of glazing
- "Section View" - orientation label
- "Obstruction is 1.5 m or higher" - callout indicating minimum obstruction height threshold
- Gray shaded area representing the primary sidelighted area in section

### Plan View (Bottom):
- "Plan View" - orientation label
- "½ × HH" - appears three times, indicating half the head height measurement used for width calculations
- "Opaque break between windows is less than 1 × HH wide" - describes the spacing condition between adjacent windows
- "primary sidelighted depth = distance to vertical obstruction" - for upper window condition
- "primary sidelighted depth = 1 × HH" - for lower window condition where no obstruction limits depth
- "primary sidelighted width = width of window + distance to obstruction on one side + ½ × HH on other side" - for upper window with asymmetric obstruction
- "primary sidelighted width = width of window + ½ × HH on each side" - for lower window with symmetric conditions
- Gray shaded areas representing primary sidelighted floor areas
- "primary sidelighted area" - legend identifier
- "EG00745B" - drawing reference number

## 4. Relationships and Connections Between Elements

### Geometric Relationships:
- **Head Height (HH)** is the fundamental measurement unit, defined as the vertical distance from the floor to the top of the glazing
- **Primary Sidelighted Width** is calculated as:
  - Base width = window width
  - Plus ½ × HH on each side (when unobstructed)
  - Or plus the distance to vertical obstruction (when obstruction is closer than ½ × HH)
  
- **Primary Sidelighted Depth** is calculated as:
  - 1 × HH measured perpendicular from the glazing (when unobstructed)
  - Or the distance to vertical obstruction (when obstruction is closer than 1 × HH)

### Window Spacing Conditions:
- When opaque breaks between windows are less than 1 × HH wide, the primary sidelighted areas of adjacent windows can overlap or merge
- The diagram shows two windows in plan view demonstrating different obstruction scenarios

### Obstruction Impact:
- Vertical obstructions must be 1.5 m or higher to affect sidelighted area calculations
- Obstructions limit both the width and depth of primary sidelighted areas
- The upper window in plan view shows an asymmetric condition where an obstruction limits extension on one side

## 5. Compliance Requirements and Technical Specifications

### Measurement Standards:
- **Minimum obstruction height**: 1.5 m - obstructions below this height do not affect daylighted area calculations
- **Width calculation factor**: ½ × HH on each side of window (maximum)
- **Depth calculation factor**: 1 × HH perpendicular to glazing (maximum)
- **Window spacing threshold**: 1 × HH - when opaque breaks are less than this width, special consideration for overlapping areas applies

### Application Rules:
- Primary sidelighted areas are calculated individually for each window
- Combined areas must not double-count overlapping regions (per Sentence 4.2.2.3.(1))
- Obstructions create boundaries that truncate the geometric calculations
- The diagram illustrates the methodology referenced in Sentences 4.2.2.3.(2) through (4)

### Design Implications:
- This figure provides the geometric basis for determining which floor areas receive adequate daylight from vertical glazing
- These calculations
